coredata.dc:title: Ratio-metric sensor to detect riboflavin via fluorescence resonance energy transfer with ultrahigh sensitivity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jilong
Su, Siheng
Wei, Junhua
Bahgi, Roya
Hope-Weeks, Louisa
Qiu, Jingjing
Wang, Shiren
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) ration-metric fluorescent probe based on heteroatom N, S doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) was developed to determine riboflavin in aqueous solutions. The ratio of two emission intensities at different wavelengths is applied to determine the concentration of riboflavin (RF). This method is more effective in reducing the background interference and fluctuation of diverse conditions. Therefore, this probe obtains high sensitivity with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.9nM (0.7ng/ml) which is in the highest level of all riboflavin detection approaches and higher than single wavelength intensity detection (1.9μM). In addition, this sensor has a high selectivity of detecting riboflavin in deionized water (pH=7) with other biochemical like amino acids. Moreover, riboflavin in aqueous solution is very sensitive to sunlight and can be degraded to lumiflavin, which is toxic. Because the N, S doped carbon dots cannot serve as an energy donor for N, S doped carbon dots and lumiflavin system, this system makes it easy to determine whether the riboflavin is degraded or not, which is first to be reported. This platform may provide possibilities to build a new and facile fluorescence resonance energy transfer based sensor to detect analytes and metamorphous analytes in aqueous solution.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Physica E: Low-dimensional Systems and Nanostructures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of N, S-CDs; (b) size distribution of N, S-CDs via Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS); (c) FT-IR spectra of citric acid, l-cysteine and N, S-CDs; (d) EDX result for N, S-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra of N, S-CDs; inset is photograph of the N, S-CDs aqueous solution under visible light (left) and 365nm UV light (right), respectively; (b) photoluminescence spectra of the N, S-CDs with different excitation wavelength; (c) the time-dependence of photoluminescence intensity of N, S-CDs measured by a FluoroMax-3 spectrofluorometer.

(a) Normalized absorption spectra of riboflavin (black line) and photoluminescence spectra of the N, S-CDs (340nm excitation, red line); (b) PL spectra of 5.31μM riboflavin, 10μg/ml N, S-CDs and mixture of 5.31μM riboflavin, 10μg/ml N, S-CDs solution; (c) PL spectra of different concentration of riboflavin (μM); (d) Raman peaks in PL spectra of different concentration of riboflavin (μM); (e) PL spectra of 10μg/ml N, S-CDs aqueous solution presence and absence of riboflavin (μM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs-based sensor (concentration: 10μg/ml) in the presence of different amounts of riboflavin; (b) fluorescence intensity ratio at 426nm and 522nm of the N, S-CDs-based sensor as a function of riboflavin concentration in DI water (pH=7) (λ exc=340nm); (c) detailed spectra of N, S-CDs-based sensor between 405nm to 450nm; (d) fluorescence intensity ratio of the N, S-CDs-based sensor at 426nm as a function of riboflavin concentration in DI water (pH=7) (λ exc=340nm).
(a) Kinetic behavior of the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs–RF system; (b) PL intensity of 0.01mg/ml N, S-CDs with 28.8μg/ml riboflavin, glycine, tyrosine and melamine.

(a) Chemical structure of riboflavin and lumifalvin; (b) normalized PL intensity of riboflavin and degraded riboflavin aqueous solution under 340nm excitation; (c) fluorescence spectra of N, S-CDs-based sensor (concentration: 0.01mgml−1) in the presence of different amounts of riboflavin under exposure for light for 3 days; (d) left: photo of N, S-CDs solution mixed with riboflavin exposure to light for 4 weeks (left) and fresh riboflavin (right) under sunlight; right: photo of N, S-CDs solution mixed with riboflavin exposure to light for 4 weeks (left) and fresh riboflavin (right) under 365nm UV light.
Scheme of N, S-CDs-based RF probe.
Quantum yield of the as-prepared N, S-CDs.

head.ce:title: Ratio-metric sensor to detect riboflavin via fluorescence resonance energy transfer with ultrahigh sensitivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas Tech University, 2500 Broadway, P.O. Box 43061, Lubbock, TX 79409, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Texas Tech University, 2500 Broadway, P.O. Box 43061, Lubbock, TX 79409, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Texas Tech University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Texas A&M University, 3131 TAMU, College Station, TX 77843-3131, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Texas A&M University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) ration-metric fluorescent probe based on heteroatom N, S doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) was developed to determine riboflavin in aqueous solutions. The ratio of two emission intensities at different wavelengths is applied to determine the concentration of riboflavin (RF). This method is more effective in reducing the background interference and fluctuation of diverse conditions. Therefore, this probe obtains high sensitivity with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 1.9nM (0.7ng/ml) which is in the highest level of all riboflavin detection approaches and higher than single wavelength intensity detection (1.9μM). In addition, this sensor has a high selectivity of detecting riboflavin in deionized water (pH=7) with other biochemical like amino acids. Moreover, riboflavin in aqueous solution is very sensitive to sunlight and can be degraded to lumiflavin, which is toxic. Because the N, S doped carbon dots cannot serve as an energy donor for N, S doped carbon dots and lumiflavin system, this system makes it easy to determine whether the riboflavin is degraded or not, which is first to be reported. This platform may provide possibilities to build a new and facile fluorescence resonance energy transfer based sensor to detect analytes and metamorphous analytes in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a nonradiative process between a donor and an acceptor. Briefly, the donor absorbs fluorescent energy to an excited state and then transfers energy to the acceptor at the ground state via long-range dipole–dipole coupling. According to Forster's theory, the efficiency of the FRET process is the quantum yield of the energy transfer transition, and is highly sensitive to many other factors like: the overlap between emission spectrum of a donor and absorbance spectrum of an acceptor, the quantum yield of the donor, and the relative orientations of two dipoles. The spatial distance between donor and acceptor is also an essential influential factor, and the FRET process is extremely sensitive to spatial distance on the nanometer scale (1–10nm) [1]. These unique characteristics of FRET allows for enormous applications in diverse fields, such as: dynamics, molecular conformation study in biological molecules, [2–4] and sensing or diagnostic systems in analytical science [5–8]. In the last decade, intensive studies have been published including the detection of bio-molecules, [9,10] ions, [11] toxic gases, [12] and even pH [12] values through FRET processing with versatile donors and acceptors.

Riboflavin (RF) is one of the water-soluble vitamins that play a vital role in functioning healthy humans. RF also plays a crucial role in many physiological activities of cells such as nucleic acid repair process, cell apoptosis and electron transfer processes in the respiratory chain [13,14]. Because RF cannot be synthesized in the human body, inadequate dietary intake may lead to fatigue, slowed growth, digestive problems, angular cheilitis and anemia. In addition, RF is susceptible to be degraded to lumiflavin, which is harmful under sunlight exposure. Therefore, it is urgent to find a method to detect the concentration of RF and to differentiate RF and lumiflavin.

Conventional approaches for the determination of RF, such as microbiological assay, [15] spectrophotometric, [16] high performance liquid chromatography (LOD=30ng/ml), [17,18] capillary electrophoresis (LOD=1.1ng/ml) [19] and immunoassay (LOD=1.07ng/ml) [20] suffer from multifarious shortcomings. The microbiological vitamin test kits are time-consuming, as they need long incubation period (44h). The other instrument detection techniques usually require high cost equipment and complicated or time-consuming preparations. In contrast to the above-mentioned methods, the FRET-based approach has many advantages including on-site testing, faster response, greater simplicity, lower cost and higher sensitivity. Currently, FRET-based sensors for bio-molecule and ions are mostly single wavelength intensity modulation type probes (turn-on or turn-off systems). However, the ratio-metric assay applies the ratio of two emission intensities at different wavelengths, which is more effective in reducing the background interference and fluctuation of diverse conditions [11, 21–26]. Carbon dots (CDs) have recently received considerable attention due to their excellent tunable optical property, superior water solubility, better photostability, easier manufacturing, lower cost and better biocompatibility [27–30]. These excellent characteristics make CDs applicable in bio-sensing and bio-imaging [11, 31–35].

Herein, a facile platform to detect RF was developed applying the spontaneous FRET process from nitrogen and sulfate co-doped CDs (N, S-CDs) to RF. In this N, S-CDs-RF system, the N, S-CDs serve as an energy donor and RF is acceptor. An extremely low LOD is obtained at 1.9nM (0.7ng/ml), which is the lowest among the RF detection method mentioned above. In addition, this sensor has highly selectivity for detecting RF in DI water (pH=7) with other biochemical like amino acids. Moreover, this method is easy to differentiate RF and luminflavin under UV light. The schematic diagram for the N, S-CDs-based RF probe is shown in Scheme 1.

Citric acid, sodium hydrate, quinine sulfate, riboflavin, glycine, tyrosine and cysteine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Dialysis bags (molecular weight cut off=2000Da) were also ordered from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents were used as received without any further purification. The water used throughout the experiments was Deionized water (DI water).

N, S-CDs were hydrothermally prepared as described previously, [36] which is widely applied to modify surface of carbon dots than other surface modification [37–41]. Briefly, 2g CA monohydrate and 1gl-cysteine were dissolved in DI water (5ml), and then this solution was dried by evaporation at 90°C. The evaporation process occurred within 4h. The resultant thick syrup was transferred into a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave, and then heated hydrothermally at 200°C for 3h. Finally, the black syrup product was then diluted to 100ml after neutralized with 1molL−1 NaOH solution. The mixture was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 2000Da) to obtain brown fluorescent N, S-CDs.

The N, S-CDs were dispersed in DI water and sonicated for 30min in an ice bath, using a bath ultrasonicator (60W, frequency 40kHz, Model 2510, Branson) to achieve uniform and stable solutions (116μg/ml). The RF solution was prepared by dissolving the required amount of RF powder in DI water. Then 431μl N, S-CDs solution was first added into a 5ml volumetric flask. After that, various specific amounts of RF solution were also added, and finally the mixture was mixed with DI water to obtain a 5ml solution. The concentration of N, S-CDs in a mixture solution was kept at 10μg/ml and the concentration of RF varied from 0.56μM to 7.44μM.

A JASCO V-550 UV–vis spectrophotometer equipped with a Peltier temperature control accessory was used to perform UV absorbance measurements. Photoluminescence measurements were performed on a FluoroMax-3 spectrofluorometer. FTIR spectra were characterized by a Nicolet IS10 FTIR spectrometer by KBr pellet method. The zeta potential and size distribution of N, S-CDs was characterized by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 Dynamic Light Scattering. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on a Hitachi 8100 (operating at 75kV). Energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) is carried on Hitachi S-4300 high resolution field emission Scanning electron microscope (SEM).

Quantum yield (QY) Measurements were used to calculate the quantum yield of N, S-CDs. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (QY=0.54) was chosen as a standard. The quantum yields of N, S-CDs in water were calculated according to the formula: Φ=Φ s(I/I s)(A/A s)(n s/n) [42]. Where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent (1.33 for water), and A is the optical density. The subscript “s” refers to the reference standard with known quantum yield.

In order to fabricate the N, S-CDs-based RF sensing system, the N, S-CDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method. The solution of N, S-CDs reveals a long-playing homogeneous phase without any obvious aggregations at room temperature, which shows good water solubility. This phenomenon is consistent with the result of an average zeta potential at −29.7mV that exhibits excellent water solubility. As shown in Fig. 1a, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image shows that N, S-CDs are well dispersed. The size distribution via Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) illustrates that the size of the N, S-CDs is in the range of 6.5–11.7nm with an average size of 8.2nm (Fig. 1b). This result is similar with the size of the N, S-CDs in the recent literatures and many other reported CDs [36,43,44]. The FT-IR spectra are shown in Fig. 1c, the stretching vibrations of S–H at 2550cm−1 and N–H at 3250cm−1 are present on FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CDs. The result demonstrates that the sulfur and nitrogen are both doped on to CDs. the vibration absorption band of C–OH at 3500cm−1, –C=C– at 1500cm−1 and –COO at 1715cm−1 are found. The vibration of C–OH and –COO for N, S-CDs indicates the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the as-synthesized N, S-CDs, because citric acid is used as the carbon resource in this approach. The elements of the N, S-CDs are further investigated by Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). As confirmed in Fig. 1d, a large amount of Si is found, which is from the substrate, while clear nitrogen oxygen and sulfur peaks are showed, which are consistent with the results of FT-IR.

The optical properties of N, S-CDs are studied by using UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2a, due to the n–π⁎ transition, an obvious peak at 345nm is observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of N, S-CDs, and a strong emission peak is found at 422nm (excitation at 345nm), which presents a good mirror symmetry between absorption peak and emission peak [45] The inset photograph demonstrates the strong blue luminescence under the illumination of UV (365nm) light even at a very low concentration (0.1mg/ml) of the N, S-CDs aqueous solution. As shown in Table 1 the photoluminescence quantum yield of N, S-CDs excited with 345nm UV light is 42.7% (choosing quinine sulfate as standard). As shown in Fig. 2b, the N, S-CDs exhibited excitation-independent emission properties, which are consistent with the results in previous literatures [34,36]. The maximum emission is indicated at λ excitation=350nm that is similar to the UV result and the emission peak is at 422nm. As shown in Fig. 2c, the result shows good photostability of N, S-CDs in aqueous solution [34,46–48.]

In order to detect RF in aqueous solution, the N, S-CDs based optical sensors are developed. The integral overlap region of the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of RF and the emission spectrum of the N, S-CDs was showed in Fig. 3a. These spectra exhibit an evident overlap between the luminescence spectrum of N, S-CDs and the absorption spectrum of RF. It indicates that the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer process from N, S-CDs to RF [9,49]. Since the large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of N, S-CDs has strong attraction to >C=O and –NH groups in riboflavin to from hydrogen bonds, which decreases the spatial distance between N, S-CDs and riboflavin that indicates the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer process from N, S-CDs [9,26]. As shown in Fig. 3b, it is clear showing that the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of N, S-CDs decreases and the PL intensity of RF increase in mixture solution, which demonstrates that the FRET process exists between N, S-CDs and RF. In addition, the Raman peak intensity of solvent (Fig. 3c and d) is stable with additional RF concentration, which indicates that the decrease of N, S-CDs PL intensity is caused by FRET process rather than optical blocking effect. The luminescence spectra of N, S-CDs at the presence of high concentrations of RF were presented in Fig. 3e. The emission intensity of N, S-CDs at 422nm is decreased with enhancing concentration of RF, which indicates that the N, S-CDs-RF system was an efficient FRET system. According to equation of FRET efficiency: E=[(F 0−F)/F 0]⁎100%, where F is the donor fluorescence intensity in absence (F 0) and presence (F) of the acceptor. At the concentration of 51.01μM, the quenching efficiency is 52.3%, and at the concentration of 76.52μM (28.8μg/ml), the quenching efficiency is 69%. So with gradual addition of RF, the energy transfer efficiency from N, S-CDs to RF increases. As shown in Fig. 4a, with gradual addition of RF, the intensity at 426nm decreases and intensity at 522nm increases. Under the specified optimal reaction conditions, the linear calibration graphs are constructed for ratio of I 522 and I 426 for different concentrations of RF samples (each concentration is analyzed three times).

From the calibration curve that is shown in Fig. 4b, the LOD of RF in N, S-CDs-based sensor has been calculated using the formula LOD=3⁎(SD)blank/S, where (SD)blank is the standard deviation of background fluorescence (in the absence of RF) and S is the slope of the calibration curve [9,50–52]. The LOD of RF in N, S-CDs-RF system is calculated to be 1.9nM (0.7ng/ml), which is the lowest among all the detection method discussed above and much lower than single wavelength intensity modulation type probes (1.9μM) (as shown in Fig. 4c and d). The result shows that ratio-metric assay is superior. Fluorescent kinetic behavior of the N, S-CDs–RF system demonstrates that the system gets equilibrium quickly and is stable at least 3h (as shown in Fig. 5a). As shown in Fig. 5b, the 28.8μg/ml glycine, tyrosine and melamine is added into 0.01mg/ml N, S-CDs solution, respectively. It is evident that there is no obvious change of PL intensity even in presence of glycine, tyrosine and melamine, which demonstrates that the N, S-CDs-based sensor obtains high selectivity.

RF is extremely sensitive to the light, especially in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 6a, RF can be photodegraded to be lumiflavin which is toxic. Fig. 6b shows that a new peak at 465nm is observed in the PL spectrum of RF aqueous solution under exposure of light for 4 weeks, which indicates that part of RF in solution has degraded. The peak at 465nm is also presented in the PL spectra of the N, S-CDs aqueous solution mixed with RF that has exposed to light for 4 weeks (as shown in Fig. 6c). The intensity of PL spectra during 422–480nm increase compared to PL intensity of N, S-CDs, which can be used to distinguish riboflavin and lumiflavin (degraded riboflavin). As shown in Fig. 6d, the color of both solutions under sunlight is very similar to differentiate. However under 365 UV light, the N, S-CDs solution mixed with riboflavin (exposure to light for 4 weeks) presents green-yellow light, whereas the N, S-CDs solution mixed with fresh riboflavin shows blue light. Therefore this method is beneficial to determine whether the RF is decomposed or not under UV light.

In summary, a novel N, S-CDs based FRET system is developed and this system can serve as a ratio-metric sensor for determination of RF in an aqueous solution. The N, S-CDs-based ratio-metric probe exhibits high selectivity and sensitivity with a low LOD, which is in the highest level among all the current RF detection approaches. Due to the light sensitivity of RF, RF is degraded into lumiflavin which is toxic. The N, S-CDs based FRET system can be applied to differentiate RF and lumiflavin, because the N, S-CDs cannot serve as energy donor in N, S-CDs and lumiflavin system. This strategy may provide a new way for constructing FRET-based sensors to detect analytes and metamorphous analytes in aqueous solution.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the support from National Science Foundation (NSF) Grant #1228127. The authors thank Dr. Brandon Weeks for the UV–vis spectra characterization. The authors would like to thank the imaging center in Texas Tech University for TEM imaging.
